text: Chrysippus, the third head of the Stoic school, identified the foundational act of cognition as the phantasia — the impression. Every perception, memory, or desire arrives first as an impression stamped on the mind, much as a signet ring leaves its mark in wax. Crucially, Chrysippus insisted that the impression itself is neutral: it carries information but carries no command. The mind does not yet feel, decide, or act. That step belongs to a separate faculty. Before the stamp can govern behavior, the rational soul must either extend synkatathesis — assent — or withhold it. The impression knocks; we alone choose whether to open the door.
question: According to Chrysippus, what must occur before an impression can govern behavior?

options.text: The impression must be repeated several times to gain strength
The rational soul must extend or withhold assent
The body must register a corresponding physical sensation
The impression must be stored in memory before it can influence action
